problems.title: Standardize --json behavior across codebase
problems.issue_body:
## Summary and motivation

Currently `--json` behavior is inconsistent:

1. `gitt miner {post,check,score}` accepts `--json-output`; every other command (`gitt issues *`, `gitt admin *`, `gitt vote *`) accepts `--json`.
2. The `miner` commands emit `{"success": false, "error": "<string>"}` on failure; the issue commands emit `{"success": false, "error": {"type": ..., "message": ...}}`.
3. Click's own parse errors (bad value, missing option, unknown option) print plain text to stderr regardless of whether `--json` was passed (but sometimes we also throw such exceptions by ourselves and then handle them specifically if in json-mode).

For consistency I suggest to pick the single flag name, unify error schema and error handling behavior.

problems.title: Replace custom validators with Click primitives
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Currently there are a lot of custom validators across the codebase, which can be implemented using Click's built-in primitives (like using `IntRange` for `validate_issue_id`).

Examples of such validators are: `validate_issue_id`, `require_valid_issue_id`, `require_valid_ss58` etc etc etc. 

## Motivation

Besides the cleanup itself, at least the following problems are also addressed by moving to Click's validators:

1. Validation is delayed past parse time. For example, `gitt admin set-owner BAD_ADDR` first calls `_resolve_contract_and_network(...)` (config load, RPC pick, possibly a contract address lookup) and only then runs `require_valid_ss58`. A typo in the SS58 string fails *after* doing infrastructure work that didn't need to happen. Same for `--bounty` and `--issue`. Moving validation onto the Click decorator rejects at parse time, before any side effect.

2. `--help` does not document accepted values. `--network` claims "(local, test, finney)" in prose but `type=str` accepts anything; `--id` says "integer" but accepts 0, negatives, and `2**63 - 1`; Migrating everything to the `Choice` / `IntRange` / etc will make `--help` the authoritative reference automatically.

problems.title: [Bug] fixed_base_score repo override skipped on issue-discovery cache-miss path - same-repo discovery rewards diverge by
problems.issue_body:
## Description

PR #1115 introduced `RepositoryConfig.fixed_base_score` and applies it in OSS scoring (`score_mirror_pr`):

```python
if repo_config.fixed_base_score is not None:
    # Only the base score is overridden. Token fields stay token-derived so
    # eligibility, pioneer, and reporting gates keep their evidence signal.
    scored.base_score = repo_config.fixed_base_score
```

The issue-discovery cache uses these already-overridden values when a miner solves another miner's issue. But on a **cache miss** (typical when the solver is a non-miner), `_resolve_solving_pr_score` in `gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/mirror_scan.py` computes the base score from files without consulting the repo override:

```python
file_changes, file_contents = mirror_files_to_legacy(
    issue.repo_full_name, solving_pr.pr_number, files_response.files
)
result = calculate_base_score_for_pr_files(file_changes, file_contents, programming_languages, token_config)
cached = CachedSolvingPR(base_score=result.base_score, token_score=result.token_score)
```

`repo_config` is reachable at the caller (`_score_miner_mirror_issues` already looks it up via `mirror_repos.get(issue.repo_full_name)`) but isn't threaded into `_resolve_solving_pr_score` and isn't consulted before constructing the `CachedSolvingPR`.

## Impact

For a repo configured with `fixed_base_score = 25`:

| Solver identity | Path | `discovery_base_score` |
|---|---|---|
| Registered miner (any) | OSS-scored first → cache hit | **25** (fixed) |
| Non-registered contributor | Cache miss → on-demand fetch + tokenize | **token-derived** (could be anywhere) |

Two miners filing equivalent issues in the same `fixed_base_score` repo can receive different discovery base scores depending on whether their issue happens to be solved by a registered miner or an outside contributor. This violates the per-repo config's stated intent ("all PRs in this repo score at this fixed value") and quietly couples discovery payout to a property invisible to the issue filer.

## Where

- Override site (correct):
  [`gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/mirror/scoring.py`](https://github.com/entrius/gittensor/blob/test/gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/mirror/scoring.py) — `score_mirror_pr`, the `if repo_config.fixed_base_score is not None` branch (around L164–L167 on `test` at commit `0cdf778`).

- Missing-override site:
  [`gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/mirror_scan.py`](https://github.com/entrius/gittensor/blob/test/gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/mirror_scan.py) — `_resolve_solving_pr_score`, the `result = calculate_base_score_for_pr_files(...)` / `cached = CachedSolvingPR(...)` lines (around L459–L464).

## Reproduction

1. Add `fixed_base_score = 25` to a mirror-enabled repo's config.
2. Have a registered miner file issue **#A** in that repo. Another registered miner's PR closes it. OSS scoring runs first, overrides the PR's `base_score` to 25, populates `solving_pr_cache` with that value. Issue discovery pick…

problems.title: [Bug] Non-added files scored as full new files when base content is unavailable in token scoring
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`calculate_token_score_from_file_changes` has no guard for non-added files whose base-side blob is unavailable. When a modified or renamed file arrives with `old_content=None` and a valid `new_content`, the call to `score_tree_diff(None, new_content, ...)` treats the old AST as empty, so every node in the head file is counted as a net addition. The miner receives a token score for the entire head file instead of only the actual diff, inflating their reward.

The das-github-mirror fetcher (`extractBlobText`) returns `null` for any base blob that is binary, over 1 MB, or unreachable from git. The legacy PAT fetcher in `github_api_tools.py` applies the same conditions. Both paths feed into the shared `calculate_token_score_from_file_changes`, so both OSS and mirror PR scoring are affected.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a mirror-enabled (or legacy) repo with a PR that modifies or renames a file whose **base-side blob** is binary, over 1 MB, or otherwise unavailable (e.g. a large generated file that a PR trims down below 1 MB).
2. The DAS fetcher stores `base_content = null`, `head_content = "<valid text>"` for that file.
3. The validator calls `calculate_token_score_from_file_changes` for that PR.
4. The modified file has `old_content=None` and `new_content="<valid source>"`.
5. `score_tree_diff(None, new_content, ext, weights)` is called — `old_content` is falsy, `old_signatures` stays an empty `Counter`, `added = new_signatures - Counter()` equals all nodes in the head file.
6. The full head-file token score is added to the miner's reward instead of the diff.

## Expected Behavior

A non-added file (`status='modified'`, `'renamed'`, etc.) with `old_content=None` should be skipped with `score=0.0`, the same way binary and oversized files are skipped via `skipped-binary` and `skipped-large`. An `added` file with `old_content=None` is a legitimate new-file contribution and must continue to score via tree-diff.

## Actual Behavior

The modified/renamed file falls through to the tree-diff branch. `score_tree_diff` receives `old_content=None`, treats it as an empty old tree, and returns a score equal to the full node count of the head file. The miner is credited for all the code in the head file rather than only their actual changes.

## Environment

- OS: N/A (validator-side logic)
- Python version: 3.12
- Commit/Version: `2087bf552f` (branch `test`)

## Additional Context

**Root cause in `calculate_token_score_from_file_changes`** (`gittensor/validator/utils/tree_sitter_scoring.py`):

The existing chain of guards handles `new_content=None` (`skipped-binary`), oversized content (`skipped-large`), and unsupported extensions (`skipped-unsupported`), but has no branch for the case where `file.status != 'added'` and `content_pair.old_content is None`. The missing guard means the scorer reaches:

```python
old_content = content_pair.old_content  # None
new_content = content_pair.new_content  # "<valid source>"
file_breakdown = score_tree_d…

problems.title: [Bug] `gitt miner post` and `gitt miner check` ignore configured network when `ws_endpoint` is also set
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`_resolve_endpoint()` in `gittensor/cli/miner_commands/helpers.py` checks `ws_endpoint` before checking the configured `network`. When both keys are present in `~/.gittensor/config.json`, a stale `ws_endpoint` silently overrides a named network the user intentionally set.

This is the same precedence bug fixed for issue commands in PR #1035, but the miner PAT command helper was not updated.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set both keys in `~/.gittensor/config.json`:
   ```json
   { "network": "finney", "ws_endpoint": "ws://127.0.0.1:9944" }
   ```
2. Run `gitt miner post` or `gitt miner check` without `--network` or `--rpc-url`.
3. The command connects to `ws://127.0.0.1:9944` instead of the finney mainnet endpoint.

## Expected Behavior

A recognized `network` value (`finney`, `test`, `local`) in config should take priority over `ws_endpoint`, matching the behavior established for issue commands in PR #1035.

## Actual Behavior

`_resolve_endpoint()` returns `ws_endpoint` first:

```python
if config_endpoint:
    return config_endpoint          # ws_endpoint wins — wrong
if config_network:
    return NETWORK_MAP.get(...)     # never reached when ws_endpoint is set
```

## Environment

- OS: N/A (CLI-side logic)
- Python version: 3.12
- Commit/Version: `2087bf552f` (branch `test`)

## Additional Context

The fix is a small precedence reorder in `_resolve_endpoint()` to match `resolve_network()` in `issue_commands/helpers.py` (lines 660–662):

```python
if config_network and config_network.lower() in NETWORK_MAP:
    return NETWORK_MAP[config_network.lower()]   # recognized network wins
if config_endpoint:
    return config_endpoint                        # custom endpoint fallback
```

Unrecognized `config_network` values (custom chains) still fall back to `ws_endpoint` as before.

problems.title: Convert per-repo `weight` from unbounded multiplier to `emission_share` (bounded pool slice)
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

`RepositoryConfig.weight` today is a per-repo multiplier folded into the per-PR multiplier pipeline. There is no upper bound on how much of total subnet emissions a single repo can attract — a high-weight repo with high PR throughput can capture an arbitrarily large share of emissions, drowning out other registered repos. This is fine while the registry is small and team-curated, but it precludes any product mode that depends on predictable per-repo allocation: repos that paid for a known emission slice, repos that lose value if a viral neighbor consumes the pool, or any future allocation surface where a registered repo expects its configured share to behave as a cap rather than a hint.

The fix is both a semantic reshape and a structural one: `weight` becomes `emission_share`, expressed as a fraction in [0, 1], with all values across registered repos summing to a value in [0, 1]. The sum is not required to equal 1.0 — if the registered repos sum to less than 1.0, the unallocated remainder is sent to the recycle UID for that round (it does not get redistributed across repos, and it does not roll forward). Each repo's `emission_share` is its allocated slice of the round's emission pool, applied at aggregation — not a per-PR multiplier. The old per-PR `repo_weight_multiplier` factor is removed from base-score and downstream score computation entirely; every PR is scored as if its repo's contribution to the multiplier chain were 1.0. Per-PR scores then determine the _within-repo_ distribution: each repo's full `emission_share × pool` is divided proportionally by score across that repo's eligible scorers (PRs on the PR side, discoveries on the issue-discovery side — see issue-discovery section for the within-repo split), so a single eligible nonzero-scored scorer absorbs the repo's entire slice, and fifty split it in proportion to their scores. Only when a repo has no eligible scorers on _both_ sides (or every eligible scorer scores zero on both sides) is its entire slice sent to the recycle UID — it does not accumulate forward to that repo's future rounds, it does not stay with the repo, and it is not redistributed across other active repos.

## Concrete example

Compare two repos: A with `weight=1.0` and 50 high-quality merges in a round, B with `weight=1.0` and 5 high-quality merges. Repo A captures ten times the emission contribution of repo B from the OSS pool, even though both have the same configured weight. Nothing in the scoring path bounds how much emission flows to either. There is no per-repo cap that says "repo A is allocated at most N% of the OSS pool, regardless of throughput."

## Root cause

The repository weight enters the scoring pipeline as a free-floating multiplier in the per-PR product, alongside label, time-decay, and review-quality factors. It's loaded from registry configuration without normalization and read by several consumers — legacy PR scoring, mirror PR scoring, issue discovery, persistence-layer queries, …

problems.title: chore: integrate pre-push vulture dead-code scanner
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Wire [vulture](https://github.com/jendrikseipp/vulture) into `.pre-commit-config.yaml` (pre-push stage) so it runs locally before every push and in the existing `lint.yml` pre-push lane and keep contributions clean of dead-code. Configure suppressions in `[tool.vulture]` for framework-driven false positives.

## Motivation

We see dead-code removal PRs quite often and it's better to just keep the contributions clean by default. `vulture` runs at pre-push prevents this from recurring: pure-AST, fast, configurable enough to suppress framework false positives without becoming a rubber stamp. Scoping it to production paths gives a second useful gate - test-only-API surface (functions defined in prod that only tests call) becomes visible too, catching drift like one-line wrappers kept around purely for `@patch` ergonomics.

problems.title: [Bug] Repo allocation ignores open-PR collateral deductions
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`finalize_miner_scores()` applies open-PR collateral to a contributor's finalized OSS score, but the repo-level allocator does not use that collateral-adjusted score when it splits a repo slice.

The current flow computes collateral in `gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/scoring.py`, subtracts it into `MinerEvaluation.total_score`, and logs the adjusted total. Later, `blend_emission_pools()` in `gittensor/validator/emission_allocation.py` ignores that adjusted field and collects PR-side allocation inputs by summing each merged PR's raw `earned_score`.

As a result, open-PR collateral changes the reported contributor score, but it does not change that contributor's share inside a repo allocation.

## Affected Code Path

- Repository: `entrius/gittensor`
- Branch/commit: `test` at `47d2651df721fc151d861cb122a353ab238351b3`
- `gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/scoring.py`
  - computes open-PR collateral
  - accumulates `evaluation.total_collateral_score`
  - subtracts collateral into `evaluation.total_score`
- `gittensor/validator/emission_allocation.py`
  - `_collect_repo_pr_scores()` sums `pr.earned_score`
  - `blend_emission_pools()` allocates from that raw per-PR sum

Relevant current code:

```python
# scoring.py
evaluation.total_collateral_score += sum(pr.collateral_score for pr in gated_open_prs)
...
earned_score = evaluation.total_score
evaluation.total_score = max(0.0, earned_score - evaluation.total_collateral_score)
```

```python
# emission_allocation.py
score = sum(
    pr.earned_score
    for pr in evaluation.merged_prs
    if pr.repository_full_name.lower() == repo_name and pr.earned_score > 0
)
```

## Steps To Reproduce

Construct two `MinerEvaluation` instances in the same repo with `ScoredPR` fixtures, call `finalize_miner_scores()`, then pass those finalized evaluations to `blend_emission_pools()`.

The minimal fixture shape is:

1. Contributor A has merged PR-side score of `120.0` after finalization.
2. Contributor B has merged PR rows totaling `80.0`.
3. Contributor B also has open-PR collateral of `20.0`, so `finalize_miner_scores()` reduces B's `MinerEvaluation.total_score` to `60.0`.
4. `blend_emission_pools()` still allocates B's repo share from the raw `80.0` PR sum.

Observed values from that fixture:

```json
{
  "actual_miner_two_repo_slice_share": 0.4,
  "expected_miner_two_share_if_total_score_used": 0.3333333333333333,
  "miner_one_total_score_after_finalize": 120.0,
  "miner_two_earned_before_collateral": 80.0,
  "miner_two_total_collateral_score": 20.0,
  "miner_two_total_score_after_finalize": 60.0,
  "raw_pr_earned_score_ratio": 0.4
}
```

## Expected Behavior

Open-PR collateral should affect the contributor's PR-side allocation inside the repo. In the proof above, Contributor B's share should be based on the collateral-adjusted `60.0` score, so B should receive `60 / (120 + 60) = 0.3333` of the active repo slice.

## Actual Behavior

Contributor B receives `0.4` of the active repo slice, …

problems.title: [Bug] Duplicate-GitHub penalty leaves cached issue_discovery_issues in repo allocation
problems.issue_body:
## Description
`_zero_for_duplicate_penalty()` clears issue-discovery scalar fields, but it does not clear `MinerEvaluation.issue_discovery_issues`.

After #1235, final rewards are allocated by `blend_emission_pools()` from per-repo PR and issue rows. The issue allocator sums `issue.discovery_earned_score` directly from `evaluation.issue_discovery_issues`, and it does not skip evaluations with `failed_reason`.

That means a duplicate-GitHub miner restored from `MinerEvaluationCache` can be penalized, have `issue_discovery_score = 0.0`, but still keep stale cached issue rows that consume issue-discovery repo allocation.

In the live forward path, `update_scores(..., blacklisted_uids=...)` later zeros penalized UIDs, but the repo slice has already been divided using their stale issue rows. This can starve legitimate issue discoverers or prevent the slice from recycling correctly.

## Affected Code

- `gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/inspections.py`
  - `_zero_for_duplicate_penalty()` clears PR lists and issue-discovery counters, but not `issue_discovery_issues`.
- `gittensor/validator/emission_allocation.py`
  - `_collect_repo_issue_discovery_scores()` reads `evaluation.issue_discovery_issues` directly.

## Steps To Reproduce

Run this on current `test`:

```bash
.venv/bin/python - <<'PY'
from gittensor.classes import Issue, MinerEvaluation
from gittensor.validator.oss_contributions.inspections import detect_and_penalize_miners_sharing_github
from gittensor.validator.emission_allocation import blend_emission_pools
from gittensor.validator.utils.load_weights import RepositoryConfig

evals = {}
for uid in (1, 2):
    ev = MinerEvaluation(uid=uid, hotkey=f"hk{uid}", github_id="shared")
    issue = Issue(number=uid, pr_number=100 + uid, repository_full_name="r/issue", title="cached")
    issue.discovery_earned_score = 10.0
    ev.issue_discovery_issues = [issue]
    ev.issue_discovery_score = 10.0
    evals[uid] = ev

penalized = detect_and_penalize_miners_sharing_github(evals)
print("penalized:", sorted(penalized))
print("scalar_scores:", [evals[u].issue_discovery_score for u in (1, 2)])
print("issue_rows_left:", [len(evals[u].issue_discovery_issues) for u in (1, 2)])

repos = {"r/issue": RepositoryConfig(emission_share=1.0, issue_discovery_share=1.0)}
uids = {0, 1, 2, 111}
rewards = blend_emission_pools(evals, repos, uids)
print(dict(zip(sorted(uids), map(float, rewards))))
PY
```

Observed output:

```text
penalized: [1, 2]
scalar_scores: [0.0, 0.0]
issue_rows_left: [1, 1]
{0: 0.0, 1: 0.45, 2: 0.45, 111: 0.1}
```

## Expected Behavior

Duplicate-penalized miners should not contribute any PR-side or issue-discovery rows to repo allocation.

After penalty:

- `issue_discovery_score` should be `0.0`
- `issue_discovery_issues` should be empty
- `_collect_repo_issue_discovery_scores()` should not see score-bearing rows from failed/penalized evaluations

## Actual Behavior

The penalty clears `issue_discovery_score` and counters, but stale `issue_di…

problems.title: [Bug] issue-discovery scoring ignores repository label policy on solving PRs
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`issue_discovery/scan.py` does not apply the repository label policy (`default_label_multiplier` / `label_multipliers`) when scoring solving PRs. `_mirror_issue_for_scoring` was written without wiring the label resolution that already existed in OSS scoring since #1027, leaving the two scoring paths semantically inconsistent.

In OSS scoring (`_resolve_trusted_scoring_label`), a solving PR labelled `refactor` in a repo configured as `refactor: 0.25` earns `base_score × 0.25 × …`. The same PR closing a miner-authored issue earns full `base_score × 1.0 × …` in issue-discovery because `_mirror_issue_for_scoring` never calls `resolve_highest_label_multiplier`.

The gap becomes a direct emission bypass for any repo whose `issue_discovery_share > 0` carries a `label_multipliers` policy, and is critical for repos with `default_label_multiplier: 0.0` — such a repo will award nonzero discovery rewards to unlabelled solving PRs that are supposed to earn nothing.

## Steps to Reproduce

Given `master_repositories.json` contains a repo with:

```json
{
  "emission_share": 0.5,
  "issue_discovery_share": 0.5,
  "trusted_label_pipeline": true,
  "default_label_multiplier": 0.0,
  "label_multipliers": { "benchmark-improvement": 1.0 }
}
```

1. A miner authors an issue in that repo.
2. The issue is closed by a solving PR with **no labels**.
3. `run_issue_discovery` scores the miner's eval.

**Expected:** `discovery_earned_score == 0.0` (no label → default multiplier 0.0).  
**Actual:** `discovery_earned_score > 0.0` (label policy silently ignored).

The same bypass applies to downweight labels: a `refactor`-labelled solving PR in a `refactor: 0.25` repo earns 100 % of its discovery score instead of 25 %.

## Expected Behavior

`discovery_earned_score` should be multiplied by the same label-policy multiplier that `earned_score` uses in OSS scoring, resolved from `solving_pr.labels` subject to the `trusted_label_pipeline` trust gate.

## Actual Behavior

`_mirror_issue_for_scoring` sets `discovery_base_score`, `discovery_time_decay_multiplier`, and `discovery_review_quality_multiplier` but never resolves a label multiplier. The scoring loop in `_score_miner_issues` (lines ~469–480) computes:

```python
issue.discovery_earned_score = round(
    issue.discovery_base_score
    * issue.discovery_time_decay_multiplier
    * issue.discovery_review_quality_multiplier
    * issue.discovery_credibility_multiplier
    * issue.discovery_open_issue_spam_multiplier,
    2,
)
```

There is no `discovery_label_multiplier` term. The `repo_config` object (including `label_multipliers` and `default_label_multiplier`) is available at the call site but is not used for label resolution.

## Environment

- OS: Linux
- Python version: 3.10+
- Commit/Version: `test` branch (post-#1235, which introduced `issue_discovery_share` and `run_issue_discovery`)

## Additional Context

**Affected repos (current config):**

| Repo | `issue_discovery_share` | Downweight labels | Seve…

problems.title: [FEATURE] Add repo allocation breakdown to `gitt miner score`
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Add a per-repository emission allocation breakdown to `gitt miner score`.

The command should continue showing the final blended reward, but also expose which repository slices contributed to that reward, how each repo slice was split between PR scoring and issue discovery, and how much reward the miner received from each side.

## Motivation

After repo-bounded emission allocation, the final reward is no longer explained by a single normalized OSS score plus issue-discovery score. Rewards are now allocated through each repository’s configured `emission_share`, with a per-repo split between PR scoring and issue discovery.

`gitt miner score` already runs the production scoring pipeline locally, but it only reports the final scalar reward. That makes it hard for miners and contributors to understand why a reward changed, whether a specific repo slice capped the result, or how much came from PRs versus issue discovery.

A repo allocation breakdown makes the local scoring tool match the current reward model and gives contributors a clear way to verify allocation behavior after changes to scoring or repository config.

problems.title: bug: subprocess-isolate scoring to close timeout-immune tree-sitter hangs
problems.issue_body:
## Summary and motivation

`gittensor/validator/utils/tree_sitter_scoring.py` calls `parser.parse(...)` then walks the AST under `collect_node_signatures`. PR #1276 set `parser.timeout_micros = 2_000_000` so the C-level parser raises after 2s on adversarial input. One class of upstream bug, which allows to "escape" the given timeout, remains uncovered and can be completely enclosed using subprocess isolation, as well as other possible cases (likely including yet undiscovered).

problems.title: fix(validator): cache issue-discovery state even when miner has zero PRs
problems.issue_body:
`MinerEvaluationCache` is anchored on the OSS (PR) phase: `store()` is gated on `miner_eval.total_prs > 0`, and `update_issue_discovery()` no-ops when no entry exists. A miner whose `merged_prs + open_prs + closed_prs` is empty after load-time filtering never gets a cache entry, so a transient `MirrorRequestError` during issue discovery has no fallback and the miner is scored zero for the round. This is asymmetric with PR+issue miners, who keep a fallback, and it disproportionately affects the only positive-issue-share repo (`entrius/allways`, `issue_discovery_share: 1.0`) where PR rewards are skipped entirely and issue discovery is the sole earning surface.

### Bug

The cache write at [`neurons/validator.py:169-172`](neurons/validator.py#L169-L172) gates on `total_prs > 0`:

```python
if not miner_eval.github_pr_fetch_failed:
    if miner_eval.total_prs > 0:
        self.evaluation_cache.store(miner_eval)
    continue
```

The issue-discovery refresh at [`gittensor/classes.py:620-630`](gittensor/classes.py#L620-L630) silently no-ops when no entry exists:

```python
def update_issue_discovery(self, evaluation: 'MinerEvaluation') -> None:
    """No-op when no entry exists for this UID — the cache only holds
    entries backed by an OSS-phase store(), so writing issue-discovery
    fields alone would leave a half-populated entry that the OSS
    fallback path could later restore."""
    existing = self._cache.get(evaluation.uid)
    if existing is None:
        return
```

So for a miner with `total_prs == 0`, `store()` is skipped and the subsequent `update_issue_discovery()` is also skipped. The cache never holds their data.

When issue discovery later fails for that miner ([`scan.py:188-204`](gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/scan.py#L188-L204)), the fallback path returns False:

```python
except MirrorRequestError as e:
    bt.logging.warning(f'├─ UID {uid}: issue fetch failed ({e}) — skipped this miner')
    _restore_issue_discovery_from_cache(evaluation, evaluation_cache)
    fetch_errors += 1
    continue
```

Inside `_restore_issue_discovery_from_cache` at [`scan.py:306-317`](gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/scan.py#L306-L317):

```python
cached = evaluation_cache.get(evaluation.uid, evaluation.hotkey, evaluation.github_id or '')
if cached is None:
    bt.logging.warning(f'├─ UID {evaluation.uid}: no cached issue-discovery evaluation available')
    return False
```

`evaluation.issue_discovery_score`, `issue_discovery_issues`, etc. stay at their dataclass defaults of `0.0` / `[]`. The miner is scored zero for the round.

The correct pattern is the symmetric one already implemented for PR-anchored miners at [`scan.py:194`](gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/scan.py#L194): they get last round's cached scores on transient failure. Issue-only miners should too.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Construct a `MinerEvaluation` with `github_id="X"`, `hotkey="hk1"`, `failed_reason=None`, `github_pr_fetch_failed=False`, and `merged_prs = open_prs = …

problems.title: [Bug] Issue-discovery drops fresh solved-issue data when the second get_miner_issues call fails (open-count fetch path)
problems.issue_body:
## Problem
`run_issue_discovery()` performs two mirror calls per miner:
1. `get_miner_issues(github_id, since=lookback)` for scoring data
2. `get_miner_issues(github_id)` for open-issue spam count

If call_1 succeeds but call_2 fails, the miner is fully skipped and cached values are restored, discarding fresh solved-issue evidence already fetched.

## Expected Behavior
If the lookback fetch succeeds, scoring should proceed using that fresh data.
If the open-count fetch fails, degrade gracefully (e.g., fallback open count), but do not discard successful solved-issue data.

## Actual Behavior
A failure in the second call triggers:
- `_restore_issue_discovery_from_cache(...)`
- `continue`
So the miner’s current-cycle issue-discovery result is replaced by stale cache data.

## Root Cause
In `gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/scan.py`, the second request (`current_response`) is treated as a hard dependency even though the primary scoring payload is already available from the first request.

## Reproduction Possibility
1. Make mirror return success for `get_miner_issues(..., since=...)`.
2. Make mirror fail for `get_miner_issues(... without since ...)`.
3. Observe logs: `open-issue count fetch failed ... skipped this miner`.
4. Miner uses cached issue-discovery fields instead of fresh solved/valid-solved results.

## Affected Areas
- `gittensor/validator/issue_discovery/scan.py` (`run_issue_discovery`, `current_response` block)

## Risk
HIGH (fairness/regression risk in reward allocation during transient mirror failures)

## Possible Solution
Treat open-issue count fetch as soft-fail:
- keep first-call scoring path,
- on second-call failure fallback to:
  - prior cached `total_open_issues`, or
  - `open_issue_count=0/derived from lookback set`,
- still compute and persist fresh solved-issue metrics.

## Additional Context
This is distinct from generic mirror-fetch-failure fallback issues because the primary scoring dataset is already successfully retrieved before the skip.

## Example with concrete numbers:

1. `2026-05-13 10:00 UTC` validator runs issue discovery for Miner A.  
2. First call succeeds: `get_miner_issues(github_id, since=...)` returns fresh data:
   - solved issues: `8`
   - valid solved issues: `6`
3. Second call fails (temporary mirror/network error): `get_miner_issues(github_id)` for open-issue count.
4. Current logic treats that as skip + cache restore.
5. Cached old values from previous round are used instead:
   - solved issues: `3`
   - valid solved issues: `2`

Result: Miner A’s real fresh progress (`8/6`) is ignored, and stale data (`3/2`) is used just because the secondary open-count request failed.

problems.title: [Bug] maintainer_cut carve-out paid to penalized miners despite zeroed scoring
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`_build_maintainer_uids_by_repo()` builds the per-repo maintainer-miner UID set without filtering miners whose `failed_reason` is set. After #1292 introduced the `maintainer_cut` carve-out and #1293 dropped the OSS `is_eligible` filter from downstream allocators, a miner penalized by `_zero_for_duplicate_penalty` (or by any other path that sets `failed_reason`) can still collect the maintainer carve-out for any repo they maintain.

The penalty zeros the miner's PR and issue-discovery scores, but `_zero_for_duplicate_penalty` deliberately preserves identity — `github_id`, `hotkey`, and `uid` stay set so the penalty is attributable in DB rows. The maintainer-UID resolver reads `github_id` from the eval and never checks `failed_reason`, so the penalized miner ends up in `maintainer_uids_by_repo`. `blend_emission_pools` then routes `maintainer_cut × repo_slice / N` to their UID — off the top, before any score-based allocation.

The penalty docstring at [`inspections.py:60-62`](https://github.com/entrius/gittensor/blob/test/gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/inspections.py#L60-L62) asserts:

> `failed_reason` is set so downstream skip-on-failure paths (issue_discovery, evaluation cache, repository cleanup) treat the miner as non-scoring this round without needing per-site penalty awareness.

`_build_maintainer_uids_by_repo` is exactly such a downstream path, but it does not honor the contract.

## Affected Code

- [`gittensor/validator/forward.py:152-158`](https://github.com/entrius/gittensor/blob/test/gittensor/validator/forward.py#L152-L158) — `_build_maintainer_uids_by_repo` builds `github_id_to_uid` with no `failed_reason` filter:

  ```python
  for uid, evaluation in miner_evaluations.items():
      if uid not in miner_uids:
          continue
      github_id = evaluation.github_id
      if github_id and github_id != '0':
          github_id_to_uid[str(github_id)] = uid
  ```

- [`gittensor/validator/emission_allocation.py:57-64`](https://github.com/entrius/gittensor/blob/test/gittensor/validator/emission_allocation.py#L57-L64) — the carve-out is routed off the top before any scoring:

  ```python
  if repo_config.maintainer_cut > 0.0 and maintainer_uids:
      carve_out = repo_config.maintainer_cut * repo_slice
      per_maintainer = carve_out / len(maintainer_uids)
      for uid in maintainer_uids:
          idx = uid_index.get(uid)
          if idx is not None:
              rewards[idx] += per_maintainer
      repo_slice -= carve_out
  ```

- [`gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/inspections.py:56-95`](https://github.com/entrius/gittensor/blob/test/gittensor/validator/oss_contributions/inspections.py#L56-L95) — `_zero_for_duplicate_penalty` zeroes scoring fields but keeps `github_id`.

## Steps to Reproduce

```python
from unittest.mock import patch

from gittensor.classes import MinerEvaluation
from gittensor.validator.forward import _build_maintainer_uids_by_repo
from gittensor.validator.oss_contributions.inspections im…

problems.title: feat(validator): per-repository eligibility
problems.issue_body:
## What

Eligibility moves from a single global gate per miner to an independent gate **per repository**. Each repo computes its own eligible/ineligible miner set from only that repo's PRs/issues, against its own thresholds — work in one repo never unlocks or penalizes another.

## Changes

- **Config**: `RepositoryConfig` gains an optional `eligibility` block — 12 overridable knobs (`min_valid_merged_prs`, `min_credibility`, spam thresholds, the issue-discovery equivalents). `resolve_eligibility()` overlays a repo's overrides onto the global default constants. The `eligibility_mode` bool is removed — "no gate" is now a repo with zeroed thresholds (`oc-1` migrated accordingly).
- **Scoring**: `finalize_miner_scores` groups PRs by repo and gates each repo independently; the gated/bypass split is gone. `MinerEvaluation` carries a per-repo `RepoEvaluation` map; the round-level scalars are rollups.
- **Issue discovery**: gates per repo, with a new `MIN_TOKEN_SCORE_FOR_VALID_ISSUE` constant mirroring `MIN_TOKEN_SCORE_FOR_BASE_SCORE`.
- **Storage**: `miner_evaluations` is written one row per `(uid, hotkey, github_id, repository_full_name)`.
- **Issue bounties**: payouts are no longer eligibility-gated — any registered miner who solves a bounty issue is paid.
- **Mulligan removed**: credibility is now plain `merged / (merged + closed)`.

## Retuned defaults

`MIN_VALID_MERGED_PRS` 5→3, `MIN_VALID_SOLVED_ISSUES` 7→3, `MIN_ISSUE_CREDIBILITY` 0.80→0.70, open-PR/open-issue spam bases 10/5→2/2. `MIN_CREDIBILITY` stays 0.80.

## Behaviour / emissions impact

This is stricter: work no longer pools across repos, so on-chain emissions reallocate the first round after deploy. Intended — see the feature plan.

## Deploy order

The matching **gittensor-db** schema change (adds `repository_full_name`, 4-tuple unique constraint) **must merge first** — the old constraint rejects multi-repo rows. **das-gittensor** API changes follow. PRs linked separately.

## Testing

`ruff`, `ruff format`, `pyright`, and `pytest` (795 passing) all green locally.

problems.title: Bump bittensor stack to 10.3.1 and drop substrate-interface
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Bump `bittensor` to 10.3.1, `bittensor-cli` to 9.21.1, `bittensor-wallet` to 4.0.1, and drop the direct `substrate-interface` dependency. The new bittensor stack already ships `async_substrate_interface`, so `substrate-interface` is redundant and can be removed from `pyproject.toml`.

## Motivation

- `bittensor 10.0.1` is ~3 minor versions behind upstream (10.3.1 is current). Staying on 10.0.x leaves us out of fixes and exposes us to the `substrate-interface` package, which upstream bittensor has migrated off of in favor of `async_substrate_interface`
- Two parallel substrate clients (`substrateinterface.SubstrateInterface` and `async_substrate_interface.SubstrateInterface`) are imported across `gittensor/cli/issue_commands/` and `gittensor/validator/issue_competitions/`. They expose the same API surface for our use, so keeping both is dead weight

problems.title: Drop density from score calculation
problems.issue_body:
Currently density is the thing which can either push your score to the sky or dump it to the ground. 

## Motivation

Density is a multiplier which is bounded in `(0, 1.15]` and it's intended to favor small changes over large, but as a side effect it also often makes really small changes score as much as a large feature scores. Even few lines of code may be enough to score much higher than a regular brand-new improvement. In pair with the fact that we still score deleted code the same as newly introduced, it also makes possible to remove a few lines of code to achieve the same results.

This question was raised previously and was handled using label multipliers. However none of existing label multipliers are currently capable of recovering the situation.

Some examples of few-line edits:
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1040, +**9** Source LOC (3 of them are comments, so could be even less), **28.97** base score, density **1.15** (capped)
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1187, -**26** Source LOC, **26.95** base score, density **1.07** (dead code removal)
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=627, +**17** Source LOC, **26.41** base score, density **1.04**
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=827, +**8** Source LOC, **23.11** base score, density **0.92**

Which are scored with bigger scores than:
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=947, + **311** Source LOC, **23.09** base score, density **0.76**
  - Fully qualified feature which has base score lower than ALL few-line edits from above. You may say that label multipliers may do the work, but consider the review quality debuff (which is more usual for meaningful changes than for few-line edits) and consider possible fix/enh label multipliers on few-line fixes as well - and voila, you're lucky if a feature is scored at least on par with what I mentioned above.
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=967, + **22**, -**27** Source LOC, **6.54** base score, density **0.25**
  - Optimization which is +- the same on size (actually a bit bigger) like PRs mentioned above, but scored x5-x4 times less.
- https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1253, **20.16** base score, **0.72** density
  - The same dead code removal, but across the whole repo. It always would be scored much lower than targeted one-function-drop PR because of density. That's why people prefer to send 5 different PRs with dead code removal rather than remove everything at once - because it will make e.g. 100 total score for them instead of 10 via a single PR. You may say that it's easier to review and land, but still: 1) you may ask to split bigger PRs yourself if you want; 2) it's definitely not fair score calculation.

Also density:
- Discourages adding/changing (specifically, as line change counts x2: +1 for addition and -1 for deletion) imports (o…

problems.title: [Bug] _is_valid_linked_issue Grants Issue Multiplier Without Checking solved_by_pr
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`_is_valid_linked_issue` validates linked issues for the PR issue multiplier, but it does not check whether the linked issue was actually solved by the PR being scored.

`MirrorLinkedIssue` already includes solver attribution:

```python
solved_by_pr: Optional[int]
```

Current validation can still accept a linked issue when `li.solved_by_pr` points to a different PR. For merged PRs, this can incorrectly grant `STANDARD_ISSUE_MULTIPLIER` or `MAINTAINER_ISSUE_MULTIPLIER` to a PR that referenced an issue but did not actually solve it.

The main reward-impacting case is when another PR closes the linked issue within the allowed `+1 day` close window after the scored PR merged. The close-window check passes, but the scored PR was not the actual solver.

Open PRs can also receive an incorrect issue multiplier during collateral calculation. This affects collateral accounting, not earned reward, because open PR collateral is subtracted from repo score.

The codebase already treats mirror `solved_by_pr` as authoritative in the issue-discovery path. This report is about making the PR issue-multiplier path use the same solver attribution instead of ignoring it.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create or load a merged PR `A` with `pr_number = 100` and `merged_at = T`.
2. Attach a linked issue with:
   - `state = "CLOSED"`
   - `state_reason = "COMPLETED"`
   - `closed_at = T + 6 hours`
   - `created_at` before the PR was created
   - `author_github_id` different from the PR author
   - `is_transferred = False`
   - `solved_by_pr = 200`
3. Call `_is_valid_linked_issue(linked_issue, pr_a)`.
4. Call `_calculate_issue_multiplier(scored_pr_a, scoring_config)`.
5. Observe that the linked issue is treated as valid even though PR `A` is not the solver.

Real production mirror payload showing the mismatch:

```text
Repository:
  entrius/gittensor-ui

Scored PR:
  pr_number = 1082
  state = MERGED
  created_at = 2026-05-12T23:12:19Z
  merged_at = 2026-05-13T01:48:50Z
  author_github_id = 75143900

Linked issue:
  number = 1081
  state = CLOSED
  state_reason = COMPLETED
  created_at = 2026-05-12T22:28:32Z
  closed_at = 2026-05-13T01:48:51Z
  author_github_id = 56657208
  is_transferred = false
  solved_by_pr = 1109
```

This is from the validator's mirror input for miner GitHub ID `75143900`.

The existing gates pass for PR `#1082`:

- the issue is not transferred
- the issue author is different from the PR author
- the issue was created before the PR was created
- the issue is `CLOSED`
- `state_reason` is `COMPLETED`
- `closed_at - merged_at` is `1 second`, which is inside the `+1 day` close window

However, the mirror also says:

```text
solved_by_pr = 1109
```

Because `_is_valid_linked_issue` does not compare `solved_by_pr` with `pr.pr_number`, PR `#1082` is currently treated as having a valid linked issue even though the validator's own mirror input attributes the solver to PR `#1109`.

This example is not meant to adjudicate whether GitHub or the mirr…

problems.title: [Bug] gitt issues list exits 0 with success=true when contract read fails
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`gitt issues list` reports a successful empty result when the contract read fails.

`read_issues_from_contract` catches `ImportError` and broad read/connect exceptions, prints a warning to stderr, and returns `[]`. `issues_list` then treats that empty list as a successful contract read.

In `--json` mode this emits:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "issue_count": 0,
  "issues": []
}
```

In human mode it prints:

```text
No issues found.
```

Both modes exit with code `0`.

This makes a failed contract/RPC read indistinguishable from a genuinely empty contract for scripts that parse JSON or check exit codes.

The same file already uses the correct pattern for a similar read-failure case: `admin info` routes contract-configuration read failure through `handle_exception(..., error_type='read_failed')`, which emits `success: false` in JSON mode and exits non-zero.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Point `gitt issues list` at an unreachable RPC endpoint:

   ```bash
   uv run gitt issues list \
     --rpc-url ws://127.0.0.1:1 \
     --contract 5GrwvaEF5zXb26Fz9rcQpDWS2Tw7Rn3myGUFcLDN7Jxxpx12 \
     --json
   ```

2. Observe that stderr prints a connection error, but stdout reports success:

   ```text
   Network: custom • Contract: 5GrwvaEF5zXb...xxpx12
   Error reading from contract: [Errno 111] Connection refused
   {
     "success": true,
     "issue_count": 0,
     "issues": []
   }
   ```

3. Observe the exit code:

   ```bash
   echo $?
   # 0
   ```

4. Repeat without `--json`:

   ```bash
   uv run gitt issues list \
     --rpc-url ws://127.0.0.1:1 \
     --contract 5GrwvaEF5zXb26Fz9rcQpDWS2Tw7Rn3myGUFcLDN7Jxxpx12
   ```

   Human mode prints the connection error and then still prints:

   ```text
   Available Issues

   No issues found.
   ```

   The exit code is also `0`.

## Expected Behavior

When the contract read fails:

- `--json` mode should emit a `success: false` error envelope and exit non-zero.
- Human mode should print the error and exit non-zero.

When the contract is reachable and genuinely has no issues, current empty-list behavior should be preserved:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "issue_count": 0,
  "issues": []
}
```

with exit code `0`.

## Actual Behavior

A failed contract read and a successful empty contract read both return:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "issue_count": 0,
  "issues": []
}
```

and exit with code `0`.

The only failure signal is a stderr warning, which is easy for non-interactive consumers to miss.

## Environment

- OS: Linux
- Python version: 3.12
- Commit/Version: current `entrius/gittensor` `test` branch, observed at `5339b36`

## Additional Context

Affected files:

- `gittensor/cli/issue_commands/helpers.py`
  - `read_issues_from_contract` catches failures and returns `[]`.
- `gittensor/cli/issue_commands/view.py`
  - `issues_list` treats that `[]` as a successful empty result.

Existing pattern to reuse:

- `gittensor/cli/issue_commands/helpers.py`
  - `handle_exception(as_json, mess…

problems.title: [Bug] `gitt miner check` reports a validator's inconclusive PAT reply (has_pat=True, pat_valid=None) as "no response"
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`gitt miner check` buckets each validator's `PatCheckSynapse` reply through `_pat_check_row_category` in `gittensor/cli/miner_commands/helpers.py:175-183`:

```python
def _pat_check_row_category(row: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    """Classify a PAT probe row; must match `miner check` table rendering order."""
    if row.get('pat_valid') is True:
        return 'valid'
    if row.get('has_pat') is False:
        return 'no_pat'
    if row.get('has_pat') is True and row.get('pat_valid') is False:
        return 'invalid_pat'
    return 'no_response'
```

There is no branch for `has_pat=True, pat_valid=None`. But that is exactly the shape the validator emits on a *transient* GitHub failure. PR #1107 (merged, `c40de7a`) added a third outcome to `handle_pat_check` in `gittensor/validator/pat_handler.py:121-129`:

```python
    synapse.has_pat = True
    # Re-validate the stored PAT
    validation = validate_github_credentials_result(uid, entry['pat'], stored_github_id=entry.get('github_id'))
    if validation.transient_failure:
        synapse.pat_valid = None
        synapse.rejection_reason = 'GitHub API temporarily unavailable; retry the check in a few minutes.'
        return synapse
```

`PatCheckSynapse.has_pat` / `pat_valid` are `Optional[bool]` (`gittensor/synapses.py:44-45`), so `None` survives serialization; `check.py:113-114` reads them with `getattr(resp, ...)`. The inconclusive reply therefore arrives as `has_pat=True, pat_valid=None` and falls through every branch above to `'no_response'` — the same bucket as a validator that never replied (`has_pat=None, pat_valid=None`).

Consequences:
- **Table mode** renders the Status column as `[yellow]— no response[/yellow]` (`_PAT_CHECK_STATUS_MARKUP`, `check.py:32-37`), which means *the validator never answered* — the opposite of what happened.
- **`--json` mode** (`check.py:130-142`): `_pat_check_aggregate_counts` (`helpers.py:186-194`) counts it under `no_response` and excludes it from `valid`, and `success` is computed as `valid_count > 0`. There is no Reason column in JSON, so machine consumers cannot tell a healthy validator (holding the PAT) from a dead one.

## Steps to Reproduce

Categorizer (pure function, no network):

```python
from gittensor.cli.miner_commands.helpers import _pat_check_row_category, _pat_check_aggregate_counts

inconclusive = {'has_pat': True, 'pat_valid': None,
                'rejection_reason': 'GitHub API temporarily unavailable; retry the check in a few minutes.'}
dead         = {'has_pat': None, 'pat_valid': None}

print(_pat_check_row_category(inconclusive))   # 'no_response'
print(_pat_check_row_category(dead))           # 'no_response'  -> indistinguishable
print(_pat_check_aggregate_counts([inconclusive]))
# {'valid': 0, 'no_pat': 0, 'invalid_pat': 0, 'no_response': 1}
```

End-to-end: with a PAT stored for the miner's UID, make GitHub's `/user` lookup fail transiently (so `get_github_identity` returns `GitHubIdentityStatus.TRANSIENT_FAILURE`).…

problems.title: [Bug] Mirror issue-discovery spam count ignores open issues older than lookback window
problems.issue_body:
## Description

Mirror issue discovery currently uses the same lookback-bounded `/issues` response for two separate responsibilities:

1. Scoring recent solved/closed issues.
2. Counting currently open issues for the open-issue spam multiplier.

Those windows should be separate. The scoring window should remain lookback-bounded, but the open-issue spam count should represent the miner's current open issue load across mirror-enabled repos, regardless of issue age.

Today `run_mirror_issue_discovery()` calls `client.get_miner_issues(..., since=lookback_date)` and derives `total_open_issues` from that same response. The mirror `/issues` endpoint filters `OPEN` issues by `created_at >= since`, so still-open issues created before `PR_LOOKBACK_DAYS` are omitted from `total_open_issues` and do not affect `calculate_open_issue_spam_multiplier()`.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Give a miner enough valid solved issues in a mirror-enabled repo to pass issue-discovery eligibility.
2. Have the same miner keep more than the allowed open-issue threshold open in mirror-enabled repos, with those open issues created before the lookback window.
3. Run mirror issue discovery.
4. Observe that `evaluation.total_open_issues` excludes those older open issues and the open-issue spam multiplier does not trip.

## Expected Behavior

Issue scoring should use the lookback-bounded issue list.

Open-issue spam counting should use the miner's current open issue count across mirror-enabled repos, independent of the scoring lookback window.

## Actual Behavior

The open-issue spam count is derived from the same lookback-bounded issue list used for scoring. Old still-open issues get a free pass because they are absent from the response used to populate `total_open_issues`.

In a local repro, six current open issues correctly zeroed the issue-discovery score when present in the response. When those same open issues were omitted by the lookback window, the miner received a non-zero issue-discovery score.

## Environment

- OS: N/A
- Python version: N/A
- Commit/Version: current `test` branch

## Additional Context

PR #796 called this out as an out-of-scope follow-up: mirror open-issue count for the spam multiplier is currently sourced from the lookback-bounded `/issues` response, so a miner with open issues older than 35 days gets a free pass.

Fix direction: keep issue scoring lookback-bounded, but source `total_open_issues` separately. Ideally the mirror serves a top-level `open_issue_count` independent of `since`; until then, the validator can use a separate current/all-time open-issue count fetch and filter it to mirror-enabled repos.

problems.title: [Bug] Extensionless configured files like Dockerfile and Makefile are skipped as unsupported
problems.issue_body:
## Description

Token/base scoring derives `FileChange.file_extension` from the basename only when the filename contains a dot. Extensionless files such as `Dockerfile` and `Makefile` therefore get `file_extension == ""`.

That empty extension causes `calculate_token_score_from_file_changes()` to treat the file as unsupported and return `skipped-unsupported`, even though `programming_languages.json` explicitly configures `dockerfile` and `makefile` as supported tree-sitter languages.

This means PRs that only or mostly modify Dockerfiles or Makefiles can be silently under-scored despite those file types being configured.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a `FileChange` for `Dockerfile` with valid Dockerfile content.
2. Run `calculate_token_score_from_file_changes()` with the normal token and language configs.
3. Observe that `file_extension == ""` and the file result uses `skipped-unsupported`.
4. Compare with `programming_languages.json`, where `dockerfile` is configured with `language: "dockerfile"`.

## Expected Behavior

Configured extensionless filenames such as `Dockerfile`, `dockerfile`, `Makefile`, and `makefile` should normalize to `dockerfile` and `makefile`, then use the configured scoring path.

Normal dotted filenames such as `build.mk` should keep their existing extension behavior, and unrelated extensionless files such as `README` should remain unsupported unless configured.

## Actual Behavior

`Dockerfile`, `dockerfile`, `Makefile`, and `makefile` normalize to an empty extension and are skipped as unsupported, producing no token/base score contribution from those files.

## Environment

- OS: N/A
- Python version: N/A
- Commit/Version: current `test` branch

## Additional Context

Relevant code:

- `gittensor/classes.py` — `FileChange._calculate_file_extension()` returns `""` for basenames without `.`
- `gittensor/validator/utils/tree_sitter_scoring.py` — uses `file.file_extension` to decide whether a file is supported
- `gittensor/validator/weights/programming_languages.json` — contains configured entries for `dockerfile` and `makefile`

Local proof before the fix:

```text
Dockerfile current ext '' method skipped-unsupported score 0.0 nodes 0
Dockerfile forced ext 'dockerfile' method tree-diff score 0.0 nodes 7
Makefile current ext '' method skipped-unsupported score 0.0 nodes 0
Makefile forced ext 'makefile' method tree-diff score 0.0 nodes 3

problems.title: Add a test suite
problems.issue_body:
**Problem**

The repository ships with zero automated tests. `package.json` has no `test` script, no test runner is configured, and `find . -name "*.test.ts"` returns nothing. Regressions in pure helpers (cost math, JSON extraction from LLM output, manifest scope classification, YAML frontmatter parsing) can ship unnoticed.

**Proposal**

Add Vitest as the test runner and a starter suite covering the project's pure, exported helpers. Keep the first PR narrow:

- No library code changes
- No filesystem / network / Anthropic SDK mocking
- No React component tests
- Just pure functions that are easy to reason about and have clear contracts

**Scope of the initial suite**

| Module | Functions |
|---|---|
| `lib/integrations/anthropic.ts` | `calcCost`, `estimateTokensFromText` |
| `lib/integrations/search.ts` | `extractJson` |
| `lib/sources.ts` | `classifyScope`, `formatBytes` |
| `lib/safe-matter.ts` | `parseMarkdown` (happy path + line-by-line fallback) |

**Acceptance criteria**

- [ ] `vitest` added as a devDependency
- [ ] `vitest.config.ts` resolves the `@/*` alias to match `tsconfig.json`
- [ ] `npm test` runs the suite once; `npm run test:watch` runs in watch mode
- [ ] Tests live under `tests/` (mirrors the existing `lib/`, `app/`, `worker/`, `scripts/` layout)
- [ ] All tests pass locally with `npm test`

**Follow-ups (separate issues / PRs)**

- GitHub Actions CI running `npm test` on push and PR
- Component / integration tests with filesystem fixtures
- ESLint configuration
- Coverage reporting and a coverage gate

problems.title: `apply_busy_delta` / `apply_event` not idempotent on restart replay
problems.issue_body:
If the validator crashes mid-block, the same block is reprocessed on restart ([allways/validator/event_watcher.py:381-413](allways/validator/event_watcher.py#L381-L413)).

`insert_swap_outcome` uses `INSERT OR REPLACE` (fine), but the busy-delta increments double — each replayed event bumps the counter again.

**Direction:** dedupe by `(block, extrinsic_index, event_index)`, or store an absolute busy count instead of applying deltas.

problems.title: TAO receive address not SS58-checksum-validated — user typo sends funds irrecoverably
problems.issue_body:
**Severity:** S0 (money-at-risk, user-error amplification)

**Location:** [allways/chain_providers/subtensor.py:276-283](allways/chain_providers/subtensor.py#L276-L283), gated by [allways/cli/swap_commands/swap.py:751](allways/cli/swap_commands/swap.py#L751)

### Bug
`SubtensorProvider.is_valid_address` only checks 48-char length and Base58 charset via regex — no SS58 checksum. The string `'xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx'` passes. The swap flow gates on `to_provider.is_valid_address(receive_address)`, so a user who mistypes their TAO destination gets past the only validation gate. The miner later sends TAO to that literal address and the funds are unrecoverable.

### Comparison
- Bitcoin has proper validation via [bitcoin.py:319-330](allways/chain_providers/bitcoin.py#L319-L330) (bech32 / base58check decode).
- The correct SS58 pattern already exists at [helpers.py:117-130](allways/cli/swap_commands/helpers.py#L117-L130) wrapping `scalecodec.ss58_decode` — but it's only used in admin commands.

### Fix
Replace the regex body of `SubtensorProvider.is_valid_address` with `scalecodec.ss58_decode(address)` wrapped in try/except. Zero behavior change for legitimate addresses; catches all typos.

The same gap exists for `user_from_address` at [swap.py:757-766](allways/cli/swap_commands/swap.py#L757-L766) but the validator's sender-mismatch check catches that one on-chain.

problems.title: SwapConfirm allows reservation hijacking by a different user
problems.issue_body:
### Problem
handle_swap_confirm() does not verify that the confirming from_address matches the from_addr stored in the active reservation.

The validator reads get_reservation_data(miner), but does not enforce that the reservation owner and the confirmer are the same address. Instead, it trusts the from_address supplied in the incoming SwapConfirm request and uses it for transaction verification and vote_initiate().

### Root Cause
The reservation already stores the user source address as from_addr, but handle_swap_confirm() never compares it against synapse.from_address.

Because that check is missing, confirmation is authorized by:
- active reservation exists for the miner
- submitted tx matches expected deposit address and amount
- submitted tx sender matches the caller-supplied from_address

It is not authorized by the address that actually created the reservation.

### Impact
A malicious user can take over another user’s active reservation.
A concrete attack flow is:
- User A reserves a miner.
- The reservation becomes active.
- User B sends the required source amount from User B’s own address.
- User B submits SwapConfirm with User B’s from_address, proof, tx hash, and payout address.
- Validators accept the confirm because the code never checks that the confirmer matches the reserved from_addr.
- Validators call vote_initiate() using User B’s addresses, even though User A created the reservation.

This breaks reservation ownership. An active reservation can be consumed by a different user than the one who reserved it, which is an authorization flaw and should be fixed urgently.

problems.title: [Bug] Repository registration rejects common GitHub repository URLs
problems.issue_body:
## Description

The repository registration form only accepts an exact root repository URL such as `https://github.com/owner/repo`. Common GitHub URLs copied from a browser can include query strings, hash fragments, or repository subpaths, such as `?tab=readme-ov-file`, `#readme`, or `/tree/main`.

The parser/validator should normalize these URLs to `owner/repo` before validation and mirror tracking checks.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open `/repository-registration`.
2. Enter a repository URL copied from GitHub with a suffix, for example `https://github.com/owner/repo?tab=readme-ov-file`.
3. Fill the rest of the form and confirm the GitHub App installation.
4. Submit the form.

## Expected Behavior

The form should normalize valid GitHub repository URLs to `owner/repo` before validation and mirror tracking checks. These should resolve to the same repository:

- `https://github.com/owner/repo`
- `https://github.com/owner/repo?tab=readme-ov-file`
- `https://github.com/owner/repo#readme`
- `https://github.com/owner/repo/tree/main`

## Actual Behavior

The form either rejects the URL or checks a malformed repo name such as `owner/repo?tab=readme-ov-file` against the mirror health response. That can incorrectly show the repository as not installed/tracked.

## Environment

- Browser: Any
- OS: Any

## Additional Context

This is a follow-up edge case in the repository registration flow. A narrow fix can parse with `URL`, extract only the first two path segments, and reject URLs that do not contain a valid owner/repo pair.

problems.title: pull request tab on watchlist page
problems.issue_body: change from only showing starred individual PRs to auto displaying all PRs for any miner or repo added to the watch list

problems.title: Repositories page: align OSS vs issue-track metrics on Repository list
problems.issue_body:
## Summary
The **Repositories** page leaderboard currently emphasizes **OSS contribution** metrics in the repo list/cards (e.g. **Total score**, **PRs**, **Contributors**). It does **not** adequately surface **issue discovery** program data for the same rows. The main work is to **add issue discovery details** to the repository list (and keep card/list consistent with the same information).
This issue tracks implementing that feature; secondary fixes (wording, `-` when another column has activity, sort/tooltips) are in service of a **clear OSS vs. issue discovery** presentation, not a substitute for it.
## Problem
- **What users see today:** The repo grid/list highlights OSS-style numbers (weight, total score, PR count, contributor count) but **issue discovery** (pro-rated / discovery-scoped issue score, solved/completed issues, issue-specific contributors) is missing or not visible in the same way for each repository.
- **Why that’s wrong:** Gittensor has **two** reward programs (OSS contributions and issue discovery). The Repositories view should make **both** programs’ signals inspectable per repo, not only OSS.

## Goals
1. **Show issue discovery details on the repository leaderboard** (cards **and** list) for each repo where data exists—at minimum: **issue (discovery) score**, **issues** (completed/solved-oriented counts per product rules, not raw open totals if that conflicts with the rest of the app), and **issue discovery contributors** (or equivalent), so they are **not confused with OSS contributors**.
2. **Visual structure:** Use a **clear two-track layout** (e.g. two bands/rows: “OSS” vs “Issue discovery / discovery track”) with distinct labels, matching the mental model used elsewhere in the app.
3. **Parity:** **Card and list** show the **same** underlying facts for the same repo; no column or card section should only exist in one mode without a good reason.
4. **Data:** Reuse existing aggregation helpers and miner-backed rollups (e.g. `buildRepoDiscoveryRollupFromMiners`, `useAllMiners`, merged-PR + discovery rules as documented in code) so numbers match the **Issue discovery** program semantics, not a duplicate of PR-only stats.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Each repository card in the grid shows **issue discovery** metrics in addition to OSS metrics, with unambiguous labels (e.g. not “score” for both without qualification).
- [ ] The **list** view exposes the same discovery fields with column headers that read clearly (e.g. **Issue score**, **Issues**, **Issue contrib.** or agreed naming).
- [ ] OSS **Contributors** and issue-**track** contributor counts are **not** the same when miners have no discovery attribution for that repo.
- [ ] Rows with PR activity do not show misleading `-` in one column when another column on the same row already shows activity (consistency of “empty” vs. “zero”).
- [ ] Sorting and chart tooltips, where they reference these fields, stay aligned with the table/card labels.
- [ ] `npm run build` passes;…

problems.title: feat(watchlist): show why each PR is in the Pull Requests tab
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

After #784, the Watchlist *Pull Requests* tab auto-shows PRs from any starred PR, watched miner, or watched repository — but the resulting list is flat. There is no visual indication of *why* each PR is shown.

Add a small **Source** indicator (a column in the list view, an inline chip group on the card view) that displays up to three badges per row, one for each source the PR matches:

- **Starred** — the user explicitly starred this PR.
- **Miner** — the PR's author is in the user's watched-miners list.
- **Repo** — the PR's repository is in the user's watched-repositories list.

A PR can match multiple sources and should show all matching badges side-by-side.

## Motivation

The most common confusion after #784 is *"why is this PR here? I don't remember starring it."* Users have to mentally cross-reference their three watchlists to figure it out, which undermines the value of auto-show.

A Source indicator directly visualizes the original ask of #784 (*"auto displaying all PRs for any miner or repo added to the watch list"*) by making the relationship explicit on every row. It also gives onboarding users and reviewers a single-screenshot understanding of how the tab works.

## Notes

This enhancement was discussed with and approved by the maintainer in chat ahead of filing.

problems.title: Make the watchlist column sortable across tables
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The ★ column shows up on a few tables — the Repositories list view (OSS and Issue Discovery), the Top Miners list, and the Watchlist Pull Requests tab. It holds the row-level watchlist star (add / remove from your watchlist). Right now the column header isn't clickable — clicking it does nothing — so there's no way to pull your starred rows to the top of the list.

I'd like clicking the ★ header to sort the table by watchlist status, the same way every other column header does. First click puts watched rows at the top; a second click flips the order. Stars added or removed should re-sort the list right away.

## Motivation

When I scan one of these long tables, the rows I care about most are the ones I've already starred. To find them today I have to scroll, search by name, or jump over to the Watchlist page. The ★ column already tells me which rows are watched, so it feels natural that clicking its header would sort by it — that's how the other columns work.

This helps in a few everyday situations:

- On the Repositories page (OSS or Issue Discovery), seeing where my tracked repos rank against the rest without leaving the leaderboard.
- On Top Miners, surfacing the miners I follow alongside the full ranking.
- On the Watchlist Pull Requests tab, grouping still-starred PRs above any I've since unstarred.

The watchlist data is already in local storage, the ★ column already exists on these tables, and the sort plumbing is already wired up for the other columns. This is mostly about plugging the ★ header into the same sort state so the click does what it visually promises.

problems.title: Add time-decay chart to PR details view
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Add a PR time-decay chart to the PR details view that visualizes how the time-decay multiplier changes after merge and shows the current scored state in a compact, easy-to-read format.

## Motivation

PR scoring already includes time decay, but the current UI does not explain how that multiplier evolves over time. Users can see the current score, but not why it is lower than the original merged value or where the PR sits on the decay curve.

A dedicated time-decay chart helps users understand:
- when decay starts
- how quickly score falls after merge
- the current multiplier and score state at a glance

This improves transparency around scoring behavior and makes PR detail pages more informative for contributors tracking earned score over time.

problems.title: Redesigning Miner Details Page
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The miner details page was originally designed as a reviewer-facing tool that enabled maintainers to evaluate a contributor's track record before merging their work. That context no longer applies. The link to miner profiles is no longer embedded in PR descriptions, so reviewers rarely land here anymore.

The page's actual audience is now miners themselves — people checking their scores, tracking tier progression, monitoring open PR risk, and understanding how their contributions translate into earnings.

UX still feels oriented toward an external evaluator rather than the person whose data it is.

All the underlying data is solid. coring breakdowns, tier unlock progress, credibility metrics, earnings estimates, per-repository performance, contribution activity, and individual PR details are all available and surfaced. The question is whether the page presents that data in the most useful, intuitive, and visually compelling way for a miner managing their own performance.

---

## Motivation

Miners care about different things at different stages — a new contributor wants to understand what they need to unlock the next tier, while an established miner wants to monitor earnings and score trends. The current page treats everyone identically.

The page also serves as the primary hub where miners contextualize their scoring. Each PR has a composite score built from multiple multipliers (repository weight, credibility, issue bonuses, time decay, uniqueness), token-level code analysis (structural vs. leaf scoring), tier-specific breakdowns, and collateral mechanics for open PRs. This scoring data exists and is partially surfaced, but could be presented in ways that help miners actually understand why they earned what they earned and what levers they can pull to improve.

The current tables (PRs, repositories) also lack meaningful filtering, sorting, and search — making it difficult for active miners to find specific data. Some of the tabular data is redundant across sections (e.g., PR table vs. repositories table surface overlapping information). Consider whether every table earns its place, and whether the data could be consolidated or made more navigable.

---

## Scope

- The miner details page and all its sub-components- Data access — filtering, sorting, and search across tables and data views
- Data presentation — all currently available API data should remain accessible; don't discard useful metrics
- Responsive behavior across desktop, tablet, and mobile viewports
- Navigation patterns to/from the page and to drill-down views should remain in tact.

API documentation is available at https://api.gittensor.io/swagger for understanding the available data and endpoints.

---

## Task

Redesign the miner details page to serve as a miner's personal performance dashboard. The page should feel like it belongs to the miner — a place they want to come back to regularly to understand their standing, track progress, and identify opportunities.

A…

problems.title: wakeword service fails while rest of stack is healthy
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

The GeniePod stack can otherwise come up healthy, but `genie-wakeword.service` remains failed.

Observed service state from the dashboard/service API:

```text
core           healthy
llm            healthy
api            healthy
health         healthy
governor       healthy
audio          healthy
whisper        healthy
homeassistant  healthy
wakeword       failed
```

This leaves the system usable through the web/API paths, but wake-word activation is broken.

## Why this matters

Wake-word should either:

1. start successfully when the local audio/wake-word dependencies are present, or
2. report a clear disabled/not-configured state when wake-word is intentionally unavailable.

Right now it shows as a failed systemd service while the rest of the stack works, which makes the dashboard look degraded and makes it unclear whether this is an audio-device issue, missing model/script issue, startup ordering issue, or expected disabled behavior.

## Suggested investigation

Collect these on Jetson after a full restart:

```bash
systemctl status genie-wakeword --no-pager --lines=80
journalctl -u genie-wakeword -n 160 --no-pager
systemctl status genie-audio genie-whisper --no-pager --lines=40
/opt/geniepod/bin/detect-audio-device.sh || true
ls -lah /opt/geniepod/bin/genie-wakeword.py /opt/geniepod/bin/genie-wake-listen.py
```

## Acceptance criteria

- `genie-wakeword.service` starts successfully when wake-word support is configured and available.
- If wake-word support is not configured or no usable audio input is found, the service/dashboard reports a clear non-failed state or actionable error.
- Restarting the stack with `genie-restart-all.sh` does not leave wakeword failed when other required services are healthy.

problems.title: GENIEPOD_AI_RUNTIME_CONTEXT=8192 destabilizes the stack under steady-state load (chat UI lag + swap engagement)
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

`GENIEPOD_AI_RUNTIME_CONTEXT=8192` (the alpha.9 default landed in PR #76, with `--int8-kv`) is destabilizing the box under normal operation. Symptoms observed on Jetson Orin Nano 8 GB with the full stack running:

- **Chat UI on `:3000` becomes unstable** — slow / hanging requests, occasional empty responses or 5xx, requests that used to be <1 s now occasionally take many seconds.
- **Other operations stutter** — `:3080` dashboard polls intermittently fail or lag, `genie-ctl status` slow, voice cycle latency banner shows higher variance than the alpha.7 baseline.
- **Swap file is in active use** — `free -h` shows non-zero `Swap: used`, which is the unambiguous signal that the working set has overflowed RAM. With swap engaged, every memory access has a chance of paging from disk, which compounds latency for *everything* (CUDA dispatch, HTTP handlers, audio capture).

## Why this is a real regression vs PR #76's verification

PR #76 raised `GENIEPOD_AI_RUNTIME_CONTEXT` from `2048` → `8192` based on issue #75's evidence that the runtime could fit 8192 tokens with INT8 KV when `genie-ai-runtime` loaded **first** (`Before=` whisper / homeassistant / genie-core). That measurement was a **cold-boot best case** — drop_caches, no resident services, then start genie-ai-runtime alone. Issue #75's table confirmed this explicitly:

| ctx | Loaded | KV cache | Runtime budget free | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4096 | 4096 | 288 MB | 1903 MB | (genie-ai-runtime first) |
| 6144 | 6144 | 432 MB | 992 MB | (genie-ai-runtime first) |
| 8192 | 8192 | 576 MB | 1616 MB | (genie-ai-runtime first) |
| 4096 | **1735** | (clamped) | (~tight) | **after full stack was already running** |

The 8192-with-1616-MB-free measurement assumed nothing else was holding RAM at runtime startup. In steady-state operation — whisper resident + genie-core's HTTP server + piper + deep-filter + Home Assistant container + dashboards + Telegram adapter + tokio runtimes — the actual free RAM after genie-ai-runtime claims its full 8192-ctx KV pool is materially less than that 1616 MB. Once free RAM drops below the kernel's reclaim watermark, swap engages, and everything degrades.

## Reproduction (representative)

On a Jetson Orin Nano 8 GB running alpha.9 main (post-PR #76), after `make deploy` + `bash /opt/geniepod/setup-jetson.sh` + reboot to ensure boot order is what PR #76 designed:

```bash
ssh aihpc@192.168.55.1
free -h        # baseline; check Swap: used == 0 immediately after boot
sudo systemctl start geniepod.target
sleep 60       # let warmup land, dashboard register, etc.
free -h        # check Swap: used after the stack is fully resident
```

Reproduces non-zero `Swap: used` and audible / measurable lag in the chat UI for me.

## Acceptance criteria

- Steady-state operation with the full default stack (genie-ai-runtime + genie-whisper + genie-core + genie-api + genie-health + genie-governor + genie-mqtt + homeassistant) on Orin Nano 8 GB does **not** engage swap durin…

problems.title: M1 exit: memory recall test set (≥ 20 cases) passes ≥ 95%
problems.issue_body:
## Goal

Prove household memory recall works reliably and **recall failures are
observable, not silent**.

## What to build

- A curated test set of **≥ 20 memory recall cases** covering shared-room
  scope, sensitivity levels, and `spoken_policy` filtering — e.g.,
  "remember X" → next session → "what did you remember about X?"
- A test runner that seeds memory, restarts the stack, asks the recall
  question, and scores hit/miss against expected content
- Failure path: when recall misses, a structured log entry must fire
  (not a silent empty context)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] ≥ 20 cases curated and committed under `tests/memory/`
- [ ] Test set passes ≥ 95% on a clean stack
- [ ] Recall miss emits a labeled log line distinguishable from a hit
- [ ] Promoted-durable filtering (`memory/MEMORY.md`) verified — shared-safe entries only

## Context

Tracks one of the five M1 exit criteria from the README roadmap. See
[`Roadmap` in README.md](../blob/main/README.md#roadmap) and the
"Memory Safety Notes" section.

problems.title: M1 exit: system prompt SHA is identical across full-stack restart
problems.issue_body:
## Goal

Prove the assembled system prompt is **deterministic across restarts** —
same config, same hydrated state → same SHA. Closes the "no silent prompt
drift between runs" requirement from M1.

## What to build

- Compute and log the SHA of the fully-assembled system prompt on every
  boot (after all hydration: persona, memory recall, tool list, channel
  context, voice mode, etc.)
- Surface the SHA in `/api/health` and `genie-ctl status`
- Add an integration test that boots the stack twice from the same config
  and confirms identical SHAs

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] System-prompt SHA logged at boot
- [ ] SHA visible via `/api/health` and `genie-ctl status`
- [ ] Test boots stack 2× from identical config → asserts identical SHA
- [ ] Any change to prompt assembly logic is now observable by SHA delta
